brief_document.title: Durable timer service: schedule, wheel arm, fire-once delivery
brief_document.purpose: Build the live tier of the two-tier timer: scheduling a durable timer records it in the event store and arms a beamr timer-wheel entry, and a wheel firing records TimerFired and delivers it to the owning workflow process. This is the microsecond live path; AT-003 adds the recovery path. The exactly-once rule that reconciles the two tiers is established here.
brief_document.task: Implement a TimerService that, given the EngineHandle seam and an EventStore, schedules a durable timer (record via schedule_timer + a TimerStarted event, then arm the wheel via the seam) and handles a wheel firing (record TimerFired, deliver TimerFired to the owning workflow process mailbox). Enforce fire-once: a timer already recorded as fired or cancelled is not fired again. Do not implement recovery polling here (AT-003) or the named/sleep API surface (AT-004) — this is the core service those build on.

requirements.title: Schedule a durable timer
requirements.spec: WHEN a durable timer is scheduled for a workflow, THE SYSTEM SHALL record it via EventStore::schedule_timer with its TimerId and fire_at AND append a TimerStarted event to the workflow history. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT consider a timer scheduled until both records succeed.
acceptance: timer_service.rs defines TimerService holding an EngineHandle and an Arc<dyn EventStore>.

schedule records the timer via EventStore::schedule_timer(workflow_id, timer_id, fire_at) and appends a TimerStarted { timer_id, fire_at } event (with the expected-sequence guard from the event model).
If either the schedule_timer call or the append fails, the operation returns a thiserror error and the caller can observe the failure (no partial silent success).
A test schedules a timer against InMemoryStore and asserts both the timer entry (visible via expired_timers at/after fire_at) and the TimerStarted event are present.

requirements.title: Arm the timer wheel for live firing
requirements.spec: WHEN a durable timer is scheduled and its owning workflow process is resident, THE SYSTEM SHALL arm a beamr timer-wheel entry (via the engine seam) for fire_at so the timer fires live without a store read.
acceptance: After recording, schedule resolves the owning workflow via the seam and, if Resident, arms a wheel entry (seam arm method) for the workflow process + TimerId + fire_at.
If the workflow is NonResident at schedule time, the timer is still recorded (R1) and is left to recovery (AT-003) — schedule does not fail on non-residency.
A test using the fake EngineHandle asserts that scheduling a timer for a Resident workflow arms exactly one wheel entry with the correct fire_at.
A test asserts that scheduling for a NonResident workflow records the timer but arms no wheel entry.

requirements.title: Fire on wheel tick: record and deliver
requirements.spec: WHEN a wheel entry fires for a timer, THE SYSTEM SHALL append a TimerFired event and deliver a TimerFired message to the owning workflow process's mailbox via the engine seam.
acceptance: A fire handler, invoked when the wheel entry elapses, appends TimerFired { timer_id } to the workflow history and then delivers a TimerFired message to the workflow process mailbox via the seam.
Delivery uses the resolved process handle; if the process became NonResident between arm and fire, the TimerFired event is still recorded so recovery/replay can deliver it (no silent drop, CO7).
A test drives a fake wheel fire and asserts a TimerFired event was appended and a TimerFired mailbox message was delivered.

requirements.title: Fire-once guard
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL fire a timer at most once. IF a timer already has a recorded TimerFired or TimerCancelled event, THEN the fire handler SHALL NOT record a second TimerFired and SHALL NOT deliver a duplicate TimerFired message.
acceptance: Before recording TimerFired, the fire handler checks whether the timer is already terminal (TimerFired or TimerCancelled recorded) for that workflow; if so it is a no-op.

The check is concurrency-safe so a wheel fire and a recovery fire (AT-003) for the same timer cannot both record TimerFired (e.g. via the append expected-sequence guard plus a re-read, or an internal dedup keyed by (workflow_id, timer_id)).
A test fires the same timer twice (simulating wheel + recovery) and asserts exactly one TimerFired event exists and exactly one mailbox message was delivered.
A test fires a timer that was already cancelled and asserts no TimerFired is recorded.

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement startup or periodic recovery polling — that is AT-003.
SHALL NOT implement start_timer/cancel_timer/sleep public surface — that is AT-004 (this brief provides the TimerService they call).
SHALL NOT define TimerStarted/TimerFired/TimerCancelled event shapes — those are aion-core; this brief records them.
SHALL NOT hardcode any interval or timeout — none are introduced here (CO10).
verification: cargo test -p aion passes, including the fire-once tests.
Timer scheduling/firing goes only through EventStore (schedule_timer/append) and the engine seam (CO8).
No #[allow], #[expect], or #[ignore] directives in new code.

checklist.text: Scheduling a durable timer records it via EventStore::schedule_timer with the timer's TimerId and fire_at, and records a TimerStarted event in the workflow history.
Scheduling a durable timer arms a beamr timer-wheel entry (via the engine seam) so the timer fires live during normal execution without a store read.
When a wheel entry fires, the service records a TimerFired event and delivers TimerFired to the owning workflow process's mailbox.
A timer is fired exactly once: a timer already recorded as TimerFired (by the wheel) is not fired again by recovery, and vice versa.

stories.text: As a workflow author, I want my workflow to sleep for an arbitrary duration and resume after a VM restart so that long delays are durable, not lost on a crash.
As the engine, I want every timer firing, signal arrival, and child outcome recorded as an aion-core Event so that replay can return the same observation without re-waiting or re-spawning.
As the engine, I want a timer fired exactly once whether it fires on the live wheel or via recovery so that replay sees one TimerFired, never two and never none.

constraints.text: unsafe_code = deny across the cluster's modules.
lib.rs/mod.rs are declarations and re-exports only.
Library errors use thiserror domain types; no anyhow in library code; no unwrap/expect in library code; lock poisoning handled explicitly.

These modules live in the aion crate. The seam with AE (workflow process lifecycle, supervision, module loading, residency resolution) is via an explicit engine-facing handle/trait this cluster consumes; this cluster does not manage process lifecycle.

Every interaction that changes what a workflow observed is recorded as an aion-core Event before/at the point of delivery; queries record nothing. No code path delivers a recordable observation without recording it (no silent observation).

Durable timers go through EventStore::schedule_timer / expired_timers; all AT event appends (TimerStarted/Fired/Cancelled, SignalReceived, ChildWorkflow*) go through the target workflow's single Recorder via the engine seam (AD CO6) — never a direct EventStore::append and no bespoke side-channel persistence outside the aion-store contract.

Tests run against aion-store's InMemoryStore; timer expiry, signal delivery + durability, query reply, child spawn, and all/race/map each have explicit test coverage. Time-dependent tests inject the clock — no wall-clock sleeps in tests.
Timer poll intervals, query timeouts, and recovery-tick cadence are engine-configured, never hardcoded defaults (per the no-assumed-defaults rule).

resolved_context.intention: Define the engine-side mechanisms that let a running workflow process interact with the outside world and coordinate concurrent work: durable timers, signal delivery, read-only queries, child-workflow spawning, and the all/race/map concurrency primitives. A workflow process sleeps for a month and wakes; an external system signals an awaiting workflow and the workflow continues in microseconds; an operator queries a live workflow without perturbing it; a parent spawns children and fans work out across the BEAM. These are the interaction surfaces, and they must be both microsecond-fast on the live path and exactly recoverable after a crash. Every interaction that changes what a workflow observed (a timer firing, a signal arriving, a child completing) is recorded as an aion-core Event so that AD's replay returns the same observation without re-waiting; every interaction that observes without changing (a query) is never recorded. The work here is the live half of durable execution — recording the observable, delivering it over beamr's native mailbox and timer wheel, and recovering it on restart — paired with AD's replay half that reads those same events back.

provenance.quote: Define the engine-side mechanisms that let a running workflow process interact with the outside world and coordinate concurrent work: durable timers, signal delivery, read-only queries, child-workflow
